comments.text: The problem as stated isn't a conjecture; there's nothing that could be proved or disproved. (The site has been updated to address this comment.)

Some problems are actually like this e.g. 271. In the case that there is no "closed form" and hence no one objectively correct estimate, I'm not actually sure what the criteria for "solved" is. Perhaps it's community dependent (if we impose strict restrictions like "estimate up to a constant factor" it may be too restrictive or too lax depending on the nature of the problem).

Even though I agree with you, the remarks do state 'A conjecture of Erdős and Moser', when no conjecture is given. On topic: as mentioned in [Be25], Choi observed that it may be assumed that $A \subset \mathbb{Z}$, so one generally sees this assumption in regards to this problem. (The site has been updated to address this comment.)

Indeed, there are many problems of this nature. I think Erdős often viewed his problems as less 'here is a precisely formulated question which I want a yes/no answer to' and more as 'prompts for investigation'. I'd like to keep this site in the same spirit. (Note it's called Erdős Problems, not Erdős Conjectures!) This means it can be a little ambiguous when to move a problem from red to green. For example, for this problem, I'd say it's when we have found the right order of growth of $g(n)$ (maybe up to lower order terms). Of course this does not mean that the problem is completely exhausted (e.g. perhaps one could go further and establish an asymptotic formula).

A result of B. Sudakov, E. Szemeredi and V. H. Vu has been left to be mentioned here, which gave the first superlogarithmic bound for this problem. Here is the link Perhaps, this will help one in getting the essence of the problem, in case someone wants to pursue this...
